coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention for their unique luminescent properties and wide-ranging applications in fields such as agronomy, advanced anti-counterfeiting, drug tracking, and intelligent sensing. This study emphasizes the sustainable transformation of agricultural waste into highly luminescent CDs and explores their versatile applications. A comprehensive characterization of the CDs was conducted using various techniques, including XRD, XPS, SEM-EDAX, FTIR, TEM, RAMAN, and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed a distinct peak around 275 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of aromatic carbon bonds. The synthesized CDs demonstrated outstanding excitation wavelength-dependent emission and a high fluorescence quantum yield of 19.68 %. The CDs exhibit exceptional selectivity towards Pb2+ ions, enabling their use as sensitive nanoprobes for detecting free Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0–250 μM, with a limit of detection (LoD) of 16.93 nM within a linear range of 0.01–50 μM. Lifetime decay experiments and UV–vis absorption studies reveal that the quenching mechanism follows a static quenching model. Furthermore, CDs derived from sugarcane bagasse were utilized as nano-priming agents for mung bean (Vigna radiata L.). The results revealed notable enhancements across all tested CDs concentrations (200–1000 ppm), including a 100 % seed germination rate, improved shoot length (23.93 cm) and root growth (17.55 cm) by the 7th day of plant growth, increased seed vigor index I (4140), total chlorophyll content (14.25 mg/g), and greater biomass accumulation compared to the control groups. CDs also prove effective in extending the shelf life of perishable commodities. When combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to form CDs@PVA, the coating effectively minimizes weight and moisture loss in fresh pear fruits during long-term storage. Over a 30-day period at room temperature (25 °C/60 ± 5% RH), CDs@PVA coatings significantly reduce fungal growth, prevent spoilage, and preserve the fruits’ natural color and appearance. In addition, the optimized CDs@5 wt% blue fluorescent ink demonstrates high efficacy in safeguarding critical data coding under 365 nm UV light. These findings underscore the vast potential of CDs as nano-priming agents for seed germination and seedling growth, anti-counterfeiting materials, food packaging solutions, and Pb2+ sensing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention for their unique luminescent properties and wide-ranging applications in fields such as agronomy, advanced anti-counterfeiting, drug tracking, and intelligent sensing. This study emphasizes the sustainable transformation of agricultural waste into highly luminescent CDs and explores their versatile applications. A comprehensive characterization of the CDs was conducted using various techniques, including XRD, XPS, SEM-EDAX, FTIR, TEM, RAMAN, and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed a distinct peak around 275 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of aromatic carbon bonds. The synthesized CDs demonstrated outstanding excitation wavelength-dependent emission and a high fluorescence quantum yield of 19.68 %. The CDs exhibit exceptional selectivity towards Pb2+ ions, enabling their use as sensitive nanoprobes for detecting free Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0–250 μM, with a limit of detection (LoD) of 16.93 nM within a linear range of 0.01–50 μM. Lifetime decay experiments and UV–vis absorption studies reveal that the quenching mechanism follows a static quenching model. Furthermore, CDs derived from sugarcane bagasse were utilized as nano-priming agents for mung bean (Vigna radiata L.). The results revealed notable enhancements across all tested CDs concentrations (200–1000 ppm), including a 100 % seed germination rate, improved shoot length (23.93 cm) and root growth (17.55 cm) by the 7th day of plant growth, increased seed vigor index I (4140), total chlorophyll content (14.25 mg/g), and greater biomass accumulation compared to the control groups. CDs also prove effective in extending the shelf life of perishable commodities. When combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to form CDs@PVA, the coating effectively minimizes weight and moisture loss in fresh pear fruits during long-term storage. Over a 30-day period at room temperature (25 °C/60 ± 5% RH), CDs@PVA coatings significantly reduce fungal growth, prevent spoilage, and preserve the fruits’ natural color and appearance. In addition, the optimized CDs@5 wt% blue fluorescent ink demonstrates high efficacy in safeguarding critical data coding under 365 nm UV light. These findings underscore the vast potential of CDs as nano-priming agents for seed germination and seedling growth, anti-counterfeiting materials, food packaging solutions, and Pb2+ sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid progress of urbanization and industrialization, heavy metal ions such as Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and As³⁺ have significantly impacted the ecological environment and human health due to their high toxicity and non-degradability [1,2]. Among these toxic heavy metal ions, Pb2+ is considered one of the most hazardous pollutants, as it is widely present in environments closely linked to human activities, including air, water, soil, and food, and can easily enter the human body through inhalation and ingestion [3]. The accumulation of Pb2+ in the human body is highly toxic, causing damage to the nervous, hematopoietic, digestive, and immune systems, as well as the kidneys. Furthermore, it induces various cellular functional changes, leading to serious health risks, particularly in children [4]. To mitigate these risks, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have established the permissible limits for Pb2+ in drinking water at 72 nM (15 ppb) and 48 nM (10 ppb), respectively [5]. Several conventional analytical techniques have been established for detecting Pb2+, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [8], among others. However, these methods have notable limitations, such as the need for expensive instruments and equipment, intricate sample preparation procedures, lengthy analysis times, and high operational costs. In contrast, fluorescence-based detection has gained significant attention among researchers due to its high sensitivity, exceptional selectivity, ease of operation, and rapid response time. Various fluorescent Pb2+ sensors have been designed using small molecules [9], DNA enzymes [10], polymers [11], and nanomaterials [12]. However, most Pb2+ sensors rely on detecting a single emission intensity, making them prone to interference from environmental conditions, concentration fluctuations, and instrumental variations [13]. In contrast, ratio metric fluorescence sensors utilize the ratio of emission intensities at two different wavelengths, enabling self-calibration through dual-channel detection to effectively minimize environmental interference. This capability significantly enhances the accuracy of metal ion detection. Therefore, the development of novel, highly selective, and multifunctional fluorescent Pb2+ probes remains a crucial research focus.

Nanomaterials have great potential applications in the field industry, agriculture and biology due to their small size and excellent properties [14]. Plant species and types of nanomaterials may cause different results (positive or negative) [15]. Most nanomaterials, such as metal nanoparticles, show inhibition effect on plants. Only a number of carbon and some metal-oxides based engineered nanomaterials have positive impact on the plant growth. In previous report, single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs and MWCNTs) could promote the growth of tomato, rice, gram and tobacco [16]. However, these investigations are limited to the study of plant growth. TiO2 nanoparticles have been found to promote spinach growth as well as improve related enzyme activity [17]. CdSe/ZnS quantum dot nanoparticles showed negative effect on the Arabidopsis thaliana [18]. CDs have attracted interest owing to their advantages in tunable fluorescent properties, easiness of surface functionalization, low toxicity in vivo and good biocompatibility [19]. In addition, CDs possess excellent solubility in water compared to other carbonaceous nanomaterials, such as SWCNTs and MWCNTs [20]. Due to these advantages, CDs exhibited promising potential in bio-sensor [21], bio-imaging [22], drug delivery [23] and gene transfection [24]. In recent report, CDs have showed positive effect on the growth of plants [25]. However, the research about other physiological effects of CDs on plants, such as photosynthesis, is rare to be reported.

Beyond sensing applications, CDs have demonstrated significant potential in agriculture, serving as nano-priming agents, nano-pesticides, and nano-fertilizers to enhance plant growth and stress resistance. Their ability to influence physiological and metabolic processes further highlights their multifunctionality. Recent advancements also suggest their role in gene silencing and photosynthesis enhancement, expanding their agricultural applications [26]. This study investigated the impact of CDs on plant growth, root development, water uptake, and photosynthesis using mung bean as a model plant due to its short growth cycle and suitability for physiological studies. CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method following established protocols [27]. Our findings showed that CDs significantly promoted mung bean sprout growth, enhancing root and stem elongation and increasing fresh biomass. Optimal CDs concentrations notably improved root system development, particularly by enhancing root vigor, which is essential for nutrient and water absorption, contributing to overall plant health. Additionally, CDs enhanced the water absorption capacity of seeds, a critical factor for seed germination, suggesting that they promote plant growth by improving root vigor and water uptake.

Further analysis revealed a significant increase in carbohydrate content in mung bean sprouts treated with CDs, indicating an influence on photosynthesis. Key photosynthetic parameters, including photosystem activity, chlorophyll content, and Rubisco activity, were examined to understand CDs' role in photosynthesis. Experimental results showed that CDs enhanced photosystem 1 (PS1) activity, likely by accelerating electron transfer via interactions with Fe-S clusters through noncovalent bonding, as confirmed by photoluminescence and lifetime measurements. However, no significant effect was observed on photosystem 2 (PS2). Additionally, CDs increased chlorophyll content, improving light absorption and photosynthetic efficiency, while also enhancing Rubisco activity, which is crucial for CO₂ fixation and carbohydrate production. These findings suggest that CDs promote carbohydrate formation by enhancing photosystem activity, chlorophyll content, and Rubisco activity, ultimately improving mung bean sprout growth and photosynthesis. This study offers insights into plant-CDs interactions and highlights CDs as a promising tool for agricultural applications [28].

Counterfeit activities have become an increasingly serious global issue, posing substantial threats to businesses, governments, and consumers. To address this challenge and ensure product authenticity, significant financial resources are allocated by governments and regulatory bodies. The advancement of anti-counterfeiting technologies plays a crucial role in simplifying authenticity verification while making replication more difficult, thereby safeguarding consumer rights. Over time, various anti-counterfeiting strategies have been developed, including laser-based anti-counterfeiting [29], ink-based anti-counterfeiting [30], and magnetic security anti-counterfeiting [31]. Among these, CDs have attracted considerable interest in anti-counterfeiting research and applications due to their exceptional properties, such as easy surface modification [32], outstanding luminescent performance [33], excellent biocompatibility [34], high fluorescence stability [35], and a straightforward synthesis process [36].

Fruits are packed with essential nutrients but are vulnerable to spoilage from metabolic water loss, oxidative browning, and microbial infestation, leading to economic losses and food contamination [37]. To combat this, strategies like low-temperature storage, modified atmosphere packaging, edible coatings, and food packaging are employed [38]. Edible coatings are particularly promising due to their low cost, safety, and ease of use [39]. This work focuses on a multifunctional edible coating using CDs to extend fruit shelf life. For pears, such coatings reduce water loss, slow ripening, and protect against microbial decay, ensuring optimal quality during storage and transportation [40].

This study synthesizes CDs from sugarcane bagasse via a hydrothermal method and evaluates their multifunctional applications. The synthesized CDs are characterized using techniques such as XRD, FTIR, and UV–vis spectroscopy. Their efficacy in Pb2+ sensing, seed priming of mung bean, fruit coating, and data security applications is assessed, highlighting their potential as versatile nanomaterials for environmental, agricultural, and industrial advancements.

Bagasse, the fibrous residue remaining after the extraction of juice from sugarcane stalks, is used as the primary raw material in this study. It is sourced from a local sugarcane juice stall (Tumkur), dried to a constant weight, and prepared for subsequent experiments. Quinine sulfate (98 %, fluorescence grade) and glucose are procured from Fluka (New York, USA). Additionally, 1,4-benzoquinone, EDTA, and isopropyl alcohol, also of 99.9 % purity, are sourced from Sigma-Aldrich. Chloride salts of various cations, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag2+, K+, Na+, Al3+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, NH4 +, and Ni2+ with ≥ 99 % purity, are also obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

The bagasse is thoroughly washed, dried, and ground into fine particles using a mixer. The synthesis procedure is carried out as follows: 1 g of bagasse is combined with 20 mL of 1 M sodium hydroxide solution. The mixture is transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product is centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove black precipitates. The resulting brown-yellow supernatant is vacuum filtered four times to eliminate residues. The filtrate is then dried using a vacuum freeze dryer, yielding bagasse-derived CDs powders, which are stored for further characterization and applications. A schematic representation of the CDs synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1.

A variety of advanced techniques are employed to characterize the CDs. A Shimadzu 7000 X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) is used to analyse the crystalline phases and amorphous content. Measurements are performed within a scanning range of 10–60°, using a step size of 0.02° and a scan rate of 2.4°/min. A VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer with a modified AlKα source and XR4 dual anode is used to examine the surface chemistry and elemental composition of the samples. FTIR spectra are obtained using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS-10 spectrometer, with a scanning range of 450 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 to identify functional groups. A confocal Raman spectrometer (STR 500) with a 532 nm excitation laser is utilized to analyse the morphology and chemical structure of the materials. The morphology and particle size of the CDs are determined using a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope operated at 200 kV. The size distribution is calculated by analysing over 30 particles using ImageJ software. EDS is employed to determine the elemental distribution and composition of the CDs. A Libra S12 UV–vis spectrophotometer is used to record absorption spectra in the range of 200–800 nm at 5 nm intervals. Fluorescence spectra were acquired using Horiba spectrofluorometer equipped with a 450 W Xenon lamp at the range of 350–700 nm with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The scanning speed is 3000 nm min−1.

The fluorescence QY of the CDs is calculated using quinine sulfate (Φst = 0.54 at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a standard, following established protocols. The QY is determined using the formula [41]:

Where, A and Ast represent the absorbance of the sample and the standard, respectively. I and Ist denote the integrated fluorescence emission intensities of the sample and the standard. Φst is the QY of the standard. η and ηst are the refractive indices of the sample and the standard. To minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance of all analyzed samples is maintained below 0.1.

Fluorescence spectra are recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. An aqueous solution containing 2 mL of CDs (5 wt%) is thoroughly mixed with varying concentrations of metal ion solutions and incubated for 1 minute. The fluorescence emission intensities for each sample are measured at the same excitation wavelength. The extent of fluorescence quenching is calculated as F0/F, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the solution without Pb2+ and F is the fluorescence intensity after Pb2+ addition. All measurements are performed at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were acquired using a Horiba spectrofluorometer equipped with a 450 W Xenon lamp. The spectra were recorded in the range of 350–700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The scanning speed was set to 3000 nm min⁻¹.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of seedlings and germination studies
ce:section.ce:para: Mung bean seeds are sterilized with a 0.1 % mercuric chloride (HgCl2) solution for 5 min to eliminate bacterial contamination. Excess HgCl2 is washed off by rinsing the seeds with deionized water for 10–15 min. The sterilized seeds are then immersed in aqueous solutions of CDs at different concentrations (200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 ppm), with seeds soaked in deionized water for 24 h serving as the control. Each petri plate contained 30 seeds and 5 mL of the respective CDs solution, ensuring consistent experimental conditions. The plates are incubated, and seed growth is assessed after 3, 5 and 7 days. Parameters such as root length, seminal roots, coleoptile, leaves, and radicles are measured using thread-based measurements. All experiments are conducted in triplicate to ensure repeatability.

Different parameters viz. germinating ability (GA), germination energy (GE), germination index (GI), germination speed (GS), mean germination time (MGT), percentage inhibition growth (PIG) and seedling vigor index (SVI) I and II have been calculated by using the following equations for mung bean [42,43]:

Where, St and Sc correspond to the total number of germinated seeds in case of CDs and control sample and Lt and Lc correspond to the length of germinated seeds in case of CDs and control. The terms ni, di and Ni are referred as germinated seeds per day, counting day and the total number of germinated seeds at the end of observation respectively. Cc and Ct refers to the average root length of seeds present in control and CDs sample. All the measurements have been carried out when the length of root and coleoptile reached to the maximum after 7 days respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of chlorophyll content in mung bean leaves
ce:section.ce:para:
The chlorophyll content in the leaves of sprouted mung bean is analysed as a function of varying concentrations of CDs in a deionized water system. Fully developed green leaves are partially hydrated by soaking in 95 % ethanol. The leaves are then ground into a fine paste and soaked in 85 % ethanol to ensure complete lysis. The resulting paste is left in the dark for 45 min, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant from each sample is collected, and its UV–vis absorption spectrum is recorded at wavelengths of 645 nm and 665 nm. The chlorophyll content is calculated using the following formulas [44]:
(10) Chlorophyll a concentration (g/L) = 0.0127A665 – 0.00269A645
(11) Chlorophyll b concentration (g/L) = 0.0229A645 – 0.00468A665
Total chlorophyll concentration = Chla + Chlb (12)

Fresh pears are sourced from a local grocery store, ensuring uniformity in size, shape, color, and texture while discarding fruits with visible defects or decay. The coating solution is prepared by dissolving 10 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 4 g of glycerol in 200 mL of distilled water. The mixture is stirred magnetically at 90 °C for 3 h. After cooling, CDs (5 wt% relative to PVA) are added to the solution, which is stirred at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C) for 15 min at 200 rpm. The solution is then degassed by stirring for 2 h at 80 rpm. The pears are cleaned, dried, and dip-coated twice in PVA, CDs@5 wt%, and PVA/CDs@5 wt% solutions. Following coating, the pears are hung on a drying device at room temperature (25 °C). Photographs are taken at intervals over 30 days to visually assess changes in the fruit’s appearance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensory evaluation and weight loss of pear
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the preservation efficacy of the CDs@PVA coating, a semi-quantitative sensory test was conducted using 10 trained panelists (aged 22–35). Pears were divided into four groups: uncoated, PVA-coated, CDs@5wt% and CDs@PVA-coated. Panelists rated color, texture, and overall acceptance on a 5-point hedonic scale (1 = poor; 5 = excellent) at Day 1, 15, and 20. Evaluations were performed under consistent lighting and temperature in a blind setup. While all groups had comparable scores on Day 1, CDs@PVA-coated pears maintained significantly higher ratings (≥4.5) for color and texture through Day 20, unlike control samples which showed rapid degradation. Overall acceptance remained highest for the CDs@PVA group, confirming the coating’s effectiveness in preserving consumer-relevant qualities.

The weight of each pear is recorded daily, and the weight loss rate is calculated using the formula [45]:
(13)
Weight loss rate
%
= ( 1 −
W
x
W
0
) × 100
Here, W0 represents the initial weight of the pear, and Wx represents its weight at a specific time during storage.

The hardness of pears is measured using a texture analyser equipped with a cylindrical probe of 10 mm diameter. The strain level is set to 20 %, and the changes in hardness are recorded over the storage period.

The antioxidant activities of PVA, CDs@5 wt%, and PVA/CDs@5 wt% nanocomposites (NCs) were evaluated by measuring the radical scavenging rates of ABTS (2,2-Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl). For the ABTS assay, a 7 mM ABTS solution was prepared by dissolving ABTS in a 2.45 mM potassium persulfate solution, followed by incubation in the dark overnight to generate free radicals. The solution was subsequently diluted with distilled water to achieve an absorbance of 0.8 ± 0.02 at 734 nm. For the DPPH assay, a 0.2 mM DPPH solution was prepared by dissolving DPPH in methanol. The antioxidant activity of CDs was assessed using ABTS and DPPH assays. ABTS and DPPH solutions were prepared in a 96-well microplate and reacted with varying concentrations of PCDs and S-PCDs (50–1000 μg/mL). Following incubation in the dark, absorbance was recorded at 734 nm for ABTS and 517 nm for DPPH using a multi-detection microplate reader. To evaluate the antioxidant activity of the composite films, 50 mg of each film sample was immersed in 10 mL of ABTS or DPPH solution and incubated in the dark for 2 h before measuring the absorbance.

SPSS 19.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was applied to analyze the significance of the data using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s test. All the figures in this paper were plotted based on the obtained data by Origin 2021 software (OriginLab Corporation, North ampton, MA, USA).

The anti-counterfeiting ink developed using CDs is evaluated for its stability and resistance to water dissolution. This ink, when applied to various surfaces, demonstrated excellent optical to prepare the AC ink, dissolve 0.7 g of PVA in 10 mL of water, then mix in 2 mL of the CDs solution. Sonicate the mixture for 30 min. The resulting solution serves as AC fluorescent ink, suitable for writing or printing on various substrates using a pen mode. The handwritten AC designs are photographed under daylight and UV light at 365 nm.

The prepared CDs were used to carry out FL quenching studies at room temperature to detect the presence of lead (Pb2+) in water samples. Solutions with varying concentrations of Pb2+ (ranging from 10 to 180 µl) were taken in the cuvette for investigations. To this 20 µl of CDs was added. Ethanol was added to dilute the solution. The PL of CDs excitation and emission intensities were observed at 661 and 676 nm respectively. The result was used for the construction of a calibration plot to carry out the analysis of lead in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of CDs on the addition of various metal ions and fluorescence stability investigations
ce:section.ce:para: The interference studies were done to understand the selectivity of the hence-developed CDs towards Pb2+. Here, aqueous solutions of several metal ions (such as Pb2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe3+ and Fe2+) were analysed. 10 µl of CDs, 10 µl of metal ion, and ethanol were taken into the cuvette for PL study. The PL spectra of CDs for the presence and absence of each of these metal ions were recorded separately at an excitation wavelength of 661 nm. The examination for fluorescence stability was conducted with respect to different pH values, ionic strengths, UV irradiation, and long storage duration. An excitation wavelength of 661 nm was directed to obtain the PL spectra for the CDs from a spectrophotometer.

The prepared CDs were employed for lead sensing in real water- effluent samples collected from sources close to the industrial areas (river water, lake water, and tap water). Here, various concentrations of lead were added to the water samples after their filtration with What man paper, and analysis was done. The spiked water samples were checked for the presence of Pb2+ions. The experiment was conducted at room temperature.

The crystallinity of the synthesized CDs is confirmed through XRD, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. A broad diffraction peak centered at 20.52° corresponds to the (002) plane, indicating the presence of a hexagonal graphite framework with disordered interlayer carbon atom packing, resulting in a turbostratic structure [46]. The amorphous nature of the synthesized CDs is evident from this broad peak. Additionally, a weak peak along the (100) plane at 44.14° suggests a partial degree of graphitization in the material. The morphology of the CDs is analyzed using SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 b, the SEM image reveals uniform spherical particles with no observable agglomeration. TEM analysis (Fig. 2 c) further confirms the uniform dispersion of spherical particles with an average diameter of 3.67 ± 0.75 nm, as shown in the size distribution plot (Fig. 2 d). HR-TEM imaging (Fig. 2 e) illustrates the lattice planes of the graphite framework, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.22 nm, indicating strong crystallinity and a well-defined structure [47]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT), inverse FFT (IFFT), and line profile analyses (Fig. 2 f) corroborate these findings, supporting the uniformity and crystallinity of the CDs.

The EDX analysis confirms the elemental composition of CDs, with carbon (52.96 wt%) as the dominant element, as shown in Fig. 3 a. A strong carbon peak observed at approximately 0.29 keV in the spectrum further indicates the successful formation of CDs, in agreement with previously reported carbon contents of 44.13 wt% and 44.58 % [48]. Nitrogen is present at 15.69 wt%, suggesting successful nitrogen doping. Oxygen, detected at around 0.51 keV with a composition of 31.35 wt%, is likely due to surface oxidation or oxygen-containing functional groups, which can significantly influence the optical and electronic behaviour of the CDs. This is consistent with earlier findings where oxygen incorporation in the range of 26–30.5 wt% was shown to enhance photoluminescence properties [49]. The results confirm the successful synthesis of high-purity CDs with minimal impurities, making them suitable for potential applications in optoelectronics and biomedicine. The green synthesis method employed here produces CDs with characteristics comparable to or better than those reported in previous studies [50].

XPS analysis was conducted to examine the elemental composition and surface chemistry of the CDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 b) displays characteristic peaks for C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, confirming the presence of these elements. The O 1 s peak is split into C–O–C/C–OH (530 eV) and O–CO (530.7 eV), as illustrated in Fig. 3 c. The above results show that hydroxyl, carboxyl, ester, amino and amide groups functionalize the sp2 domain of CDs [48]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3 d) exhibits peaks at 399.5, 400.5, and 401.9 eV, attributable to C–NH–C and –NH2 groups The deconvoluted N 1 s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks, corresponding to different nitrogen functionalities. The first peak, observed at ∼399.5 eV, is assigned to pyridinic-N, where nitrogen is bonded to two sp² hybridized carbon atoms at the edge of the graphene-like structure, forming a CN bond. Pyridinic nitrogen introduces localized electron density. The second peak, appearing at ∼400.5 eV, corresponds to pyrrolic-N, where nitrogen is incorporated into a five-membered ring structure, forming C–N bonds similar to pyrrole. The third peak, centered at ∼401.9 eV, is attributed to graphitic-N, where nitrogen is substituted within the carbon lattice, forming C–N–C bonds [51]. The presence of these nitrogen species confirms effective doping, enhancing the CDs' surface reactivity, fluorescence efficiency, and metal ion binding capacity, making them promising candidates for Pb2+ sensing and photocatalysis. Fig. 3 e presents the C 1 s XPS spectrum, revealing three peaks at 285.1, 286.6 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to CC, C–OH and CO functional groups [52], respectively. The CC peak indicates a graphitic carbon framework, enhancing electronic conductivity. The C–OH peak confirms oxygen-containing groups that improve hydrophilicity and dispersion, while the CO peak supports surface oxidation. These functional groups contribute to the fluorescence properties, reactivity, and sensing efficiency of the CDs, making them suitable for environmental and biomedical applications.

FTIR spectroscopy confirmed the presence of various functional groups in the CDs, providing insight into their chemical composition and surface properties. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the broad peak at 3254 cm⁻¹ corresponds to hydroxyl (–OH) stretching, contributing to the hydrophilicity and aqueous dispersibility of the CDs. The 2928 cm⁻¹ peak is attributed to C–H stretching from aliphatic hydrocarbons or sp³ -hybridized carbon. The 1713 cm⁻¹ peak indicates carbonyl (CO) stretching, suggesting carboxyl or amide groups that enhance interactions with metal ions and biomolecules. The 1020 cm⁻¹ peak corresponds to C–N and/or C–O stretching, signifying oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, while the 832 cm⁻¹ peak is associated with N–H bending, confirming amine or amide groups. Additionally, peaks at 1419 cm⁻¹ and 1375 cm⁻¹ indicate C–H bending and C–N stretching, respectively [53,54]. These functional groups improve the water solubility and surface reactivity of CDs, making them suitable for applications such as metal ion sensing, bioimaging, and catalysis.

The Raman spectrum of CDs displays two prominent peaks at 1590 cm⁻¹ (G-band) and 1345 cm⁻¹ (D-band) (Fig. 4 b). The G-band corresponds to sp² hybridized graphitic domains, while the D-band originates from structural defects, edge effects, and disorder in the carbon framework, often associated with sp³ hybridization. The ID/IG ratio of 1.01 indicates a relatively high degree of disorder compared to fully graphitized carbon but still retains significant graphitic characteristics. The enhanced D-band intensity suggests the presence of defects, oxygen-containing functional groups, and amorphous carbon regions, which can improve surface reactivity, fluorescence properties, and functionalization potential [55]. The near-unity ID/IG value confirms that the CDs possess a balanced combination of ordered graphitic and defect-rich domains, aligning with the reported characteristics of high-quality CDs.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs are investigated to assess their optical properties. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 c) shows a prominent peak at approximately 275 nm, attributed to the π–π* transition of aromatic carbon bonds [56]. The excitation spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 4 d) displays a maximum excitation wavelength (λex) at 360 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength (λem) at 440 nm. The chromaticity coordinates (CIE) of the CDs are calculated and found to be (0.148, 0.109), indicating a blue fluorescence (Fig. 4 e). Furthermore, the emission spectra of the CDs exhibited excitation-dependent behaviour (Fig. 4 f). As the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 390 nm, the emission band intensity initially increased, followed by a redshift from 430 to 481 nm. This behaviour reflects the diverse emissive states within the CDs. The QY of the CDs is determined using the UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of quinine sulphate (QS) as a reference. The QY of the CDs is calculated to be 19.68 %, indicating their high fluorescence efficiency and suitability for various optical applications.

The stability of the CDs is evaluated through fluorescence intensity measurements under various conditions. To assess long-term stability, fluorescence intensity is monitored over 200 days at intervals of 50 days (Figure S1a & b). Additionally, the stability under UV light exposure is tested for 120 min, with measurements taken every 30 min (Figure S1c & d). The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity remained largely unchanged over time, demonstrating the excellent stability of the synthesized CDs. The fluorescent behavior of CDs is further analyzed under different pH conditions to explore their potential as biomarkers in diverse biological environments. Stability studies are conducted across a pH range of 3–11 (Figure S1e & f). The results revealed that fluorescence intensity is significantly affected by pH changes, showing quenching in both strongly acidic (pH ∼3) and strongly basic (pH ∼11) conditions. The maximum fluorescence intensity is observed at neutral pH (pH ∼7), which closely aligns with physiological conditions. This suggests that the CDs are well-suited for applications in bio-imaging and biosensors. The pH-sensitive fluorescence behavior of the CDs can be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface. In acidic environments, protonation may reduce the availability of functional groups, while in basic environments, deprotonation may alter the electronic structure, leading to fluorescence quenching. These findings highlight the versatility of CDs in stable and controlled biological applications [57].

The stability of the AC ink was evaluated under low (<30 % RH), moderate (30–60 % RH), and high (>60 % RH) humidity conditions using a controlled humidity chamber. The relative humidity (RH) was precisely maintained and monitored using a hygrometer to assess the ink’s response to varying moisture levels. The obtained results are presented in Figure S2, illustrating the AC images under these three humidity conditions. Under low humidity conditions (< 30 % RH), the AC ink exhibited minimal or no visible changes, maintaining its original color and fluorescence properties as show in Figure S2 a & b. This suggests that the ink remains stable in dry environments, with little interaction with moisture. However, minor fading or reduced intensity was observed in some cases, possibly due to reduced molecular interactions with ambient moisture. At moderate humidity levels (30–60 % RH), the ink retained its optimal visibility and fluorescence, indicating that it performs well under typical environmental conditions as show in Figure S2 c & d. The AC features remained clear and distinguishable, demonstrating the ink’s stability and reliability for practical applications in standard storage and operational settings. In high humidity conditions (> 60 % RH), noticeable changes in the AC ink’s appearance were observed (Figure S2e & f). Increased humidity led to enhanced fluorescence intensity or color shifts, likely due to the ink’s interaction with moisture-sensitive functional groups. Some variations in the AC images suggest activation of humidity-responsive properties, making the ink useful for security applications where authentication is required under specific environmental triggers. The PL intensity was monitored at 0, 5, 10, 15 and 20 days of exposure. Throughout the testing period, the samples exhibited stable emission properties under low and moderate humidity conditions, with minimal changes in PL intensity. However, under high relative humidity (80 %), a slight reduction in PL intensity (∼12 %) was observed after 20 days, likely due to surface moisture adsorption. However, prolonged exposure to high humidity may lead to diffusion effects or degradation, which should be considered for long-term stability. Overall, the results indicate that the AC ink remains stable under low and moderate humidity conditions, with significant fluorescence enhancement or response under high humidity, highlighting its potential for advanced anticounterfeiting applications [58].

The sensitivity of the sensor system is evaluated by monitoring fluorescence changes upon adding varying concentrations of Pb2+ ions (0–250 µM). As the concentration of Pb2+ ions increased, a gradual quenching of the blue fluorescence at 440 nm is observed ( Fig. 5 a). This demonstrates the excellent sensitivity of CDs towards Pb2+ ions. The quenching mechanism is attributed to the interaction of Pb2+ ions with surface functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, present on the CDs. Among these, oxygen-containing groups exhibit a higher binding affinity for Pb2+ ions. Fluorescence quenching occurs due to the formation of stable Pb–O bonds between Pb2+ ions and the CDs [59]. A linear relationship is observed between the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) and Pb2+ concentrations within the range of 0.01–50 µM (Fig. 5 b). Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the CDs without and with Pb2+ ions, respectively. The analysis is performed in triplicate to ensure reliability. The LoD is calculated using the 3σ/s method, where σ is the standard deviation of the measurements and s is the slope of the calibration curve. The LoD is determined to be 16.9 nM, which is notably lower than those reported in previous studies, as summarized in Table 1 [60–67]. This exceptional sensitivity highlights the potential of CDs for Pb2+ detection in trace concentrations.

To assess the selectivity of CDs as a fluorescent probe, the emission response is tested against several metal ions. Figure S3a shows that 10 µM of Pb2+ significantly quenched the fluorescence emission, while 50 µM of other metal ions including Mn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ exhibited negligible effects on fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5 d). Photographs of ion sensing are provided in Fig. 5 c. These findings demonstrate the strong selectivity of CDs for Pb2+ ions. The exceptional selectivity arises from the competitive binding of free –COOH and –OH groups on the CDs’ surface, with Pb2+ forming stable interactions. This strong binding facilitates non-radiative recombination of excitons via an electron transfer mechanism [68].

Interference tests are conducted to evaluate the effect of co-existing metal ions on Pb2+ detection (Fig. 5 e). As shown in Figure S3b, the quenching efficiency remained unaffected in the presence of other metal ions, indicating the robustness of the sensor. While Fe3+ caused slight fluorescence quenching, this can be attributed to its minor affinity for the hydroxyl or amino groups on the CDs’ surface. Interestingly, the CDs did not exhibit any response to Fe2+. This interference from Fe3+ can be mitigated by reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ using ascorbic acid [69]. These results demonstrate that the designed fluorescent probe offers a highly selective and interference-free platform for detecting Pb2+ ions, making it an ideal candidate for environmental and biological sensing applications.

Pb2+ ions exhibit a high affinity for oxygen-containing functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, which play a crucial role in their interaction with CDs. The fluorescence quenching observed in the CDs upon Pb2+ addition can be attributed to specific coordination between the fluorophore (CDs) and the quencher (Pb2+ ions). This interaction determines whether the quenching mechanism is static, dynamic, or a combination of both [70]. To differentiate between these mechanisms, the sensitivity of quenching to parameters like viscosity, temperature, or fluorescence lifetime is examined. Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) studies are conducted by exciting the CDs with a 380 nm laser in the presence of varying concentrations of Pb2+ ions ( Fig. 6 a & b). The fluorescence decay data are analyzed using a bi-exponential equation [71]:
(14) I = A1 exp(-t/τ1) + A2 exp(-t/τ2)

Here, I(t) is the fluorescence intensity at time t, and τ1 and τ2 are the decay time components with corresponding fitting constants A1 and A2. The average fluorescence lifetime (τavg) is calculated using the following equation [72]:
(15)

The lifetime of the CDs is found to be 0.587 ns, which increased slightly to 0.595 ns after adding 10 µM of Pb2+ ions. This negligible change in lifetime suggests a static quenching mechanism, where the quenching results from the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between the CDs and Pb2+ ions. Further confirmation is obtained using the Stern-Volmer equation [73]:
(16)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
K
q
τ
0
Q
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs in the absence and presence of Pb2+ ions, respectively; [Q] is the concentration of Pb2+; KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant; Kq is the quenching constant; and τ0 is the lifetime of the CDs without Pb2+ (0.587 ns). The Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) is determined to be 2.01 × 105 Lmol−1, and the quenching constant (Kq) is calculated as 3.42 × 1014 Lmol−1s−1 (Fig. 6 c). This value is significantly higher than the maximum value for dynamic quenching (2.0 ×1010 Lmol−1s−1) [74], further confirming a static quenching mechanism. To investigate the quenching further, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is performed. The absorption peak intensity at 270 nm decreased progressively with increasing concentrations of Pb2+ ions (Fig. 6 d), indicating the formation of a ground-state complex between the CDs and Pb2+ ions [75].

The fluorescence quenching is reversible, as demonstrated by adding CaNa2EDTA (100 µM) to the Pb2+-CDs solution. Upon addition, fluorescence intensity is restored (Fig. 6 e & f). This recovery occurs because Pb2+ ions preferentially form a stable complex with EDTA ([Pb(EDTA)]−2), releasing them from the CDs [76]. The interaction between Pb2+ ions and CDs is predominantly governed by oxygen-containing functional groups, such as –COOH and –OH, which bind strongly to Pb2+ ions. This mechanism mirrors the principle underlying lead chelation therapy, where CaNa2EDTA, a hexadentate ligand with oxygen and nitrogen donor sites, is used to remove Pb2+ ions from the body. The binding between these functional groups and Pb2+ ions disrupts the fluorescence characteristics of the CDs, resulting in quenching [77]. A schematic illustration of the interaction between CDs and Pb2+ ions, along with fluorescence recovery using EDTA, is shown in Fig. 6 g.

We have expanded the selectivity study by including the analysis of lead content in various wastewater effluents and conducting fluorescence detection of Pb2+ ions in real water samples. This addition strengthens the study by demonstrating the practical applicability of our method beyond controlled laboratory conditions. To evaluate the practicality and effectiveness of the fluorometric sensors, they were applied for Pb2+ detection in real water samples. The prepared fluorescent CDs were utilized to detect Pb2+ ions in wastewater samples collected from three different sources: river water, tap water, and lake water. These samples were obtained from areas near industrial zones, where lead contamination is more likely due to industrial discharges. Prior to analysis, the water samples underwent filtration using Whatman filter paper to remove any suspended particles and impurities that could interfere with the fluorescence measurements. After filtration, varying concentrations of Pb2+ were spiked into the water samples to simulate different contamination levels. The proposed fluorometric detection method was then applied to measure the lead content. The Figure S4 presents a comparison of the fluorescence quenching efficiency by Pb2+ ions at different concentrations across the water samples. A clear trend of decreasing fluorescence intensity was observed as the Pb2+ concentration increased, indicating effective quenching behaviour. This reduction in fluorescence is attributed to the interaction between the Pb2+ ions and the surface functional groups of the CDs, which induces a static quenching mechanism. The recovery values, ranging from 99 % to 101 %, demonstrate the high accuracy and reliability of the detection method across various water samples. These values indicate that the method produces consistent and reproducible results, making it suitable for real-world applications. The synthesized fluorescent CDs were successfully employed for lead detection in real water samples, as summarized in Table 2. This confirms the applicability of the proposed method for environmental monitoring and wastewater analysis.

To evaluate the potential of CDs in promoting seed germination and seedling development, mung bean seeds are selected as the model plant. The seeds are primed in CDs@5 wt% solutions at varying concentrations (200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 ppm) with Milli-Q water serving as the control. Germination success is observed within 2 days for all groups, and the seed germination rate is monitored every 6 h until 36 h after sowing in Petri dishes. A clear difference in seedling phenotype is observed between the control group and those treated with CDs ( Fig. 7). As shown in Fig. 8 a and b , water-primed seeds exhibited the lowest germination ability, energy and index, while seeds treated with CDs particularly at 600 ppm showed significant improvements [78]. The obtained values of germination parameters for all the groups are summarized in Table 3. The results demonstrated that CDs affect the germination ability and the energy of germination when compared with the control group. This ability increased until the concentration reached 600 ppm, and then it dropped slightly further. For 600 ppm concentration of CDs, both the germination ability and energy enhancement were observed with a standard error of the mean (SE of Mean) of 3.1818 and 5.8158, respectively, for germination ability and germination energy with an F-value and p-value of 7.19334 and 0.0065 (<0.05), respectively.

After only 8 h of sowing, 50 % of the seeds treated with the 600 ppm CDs solution exhibited radicle protrusion, whereas the control group had a much slower germination rate of 21.6 %. Other CDs-treated groups (200, 400, 800, and 1000 ppm) demonstrated comparable germination rates, all of which are notably higher than the control within the first 40 h. The mean germination time for the 600-ppm group is 1.06 days, approximately 1.5 times faster than the control (Table 3 a). These results indicate that CDs can significantly accelerate seed germination through nano-priming, laying a strong foundation for subsequent seedling development. A significant enhancement in initial root length is observed for the CDs-treated seeds compared to the control group. By Day 3, radicles from control seeds are visibly shorter and finer than those from CDs-treated seeds. Measurements of seedling phenotypes, including length, diameter, biomass, and moisture content, are taken after harvest [79].

All CDs-treated groups (200–1000 ppm) showed better seedling growth than the control, with a dose-dependent trend up to 600 ppm. As shown in Fig. 8 c, shoot and root lengths increased with CDs concentration, peaking at 600 ppm before slightly declining at 1000 ppm. Notably, 600 ppm CDs treatment significantly increased shoot and root lengths compared to the control. Shoot diameter at 900 ppm is 1.19 times greater than the control (Fig. 8 d), while root diameters are consistently larger across all CDs-treated groups, indicating the development of a stronger root system essential for seedling growth. Biomass accumulation followed a similar pattern. These results confirm that CDs priming accelerates biomass accumulation, a critical indicator of plant growth [80]. The shoot and root lengths and diameters of seedlings exhibited a growing trend until the CD concentration for seed priming reached 600 ppm. Then, it slightly decreased when the concentrations increased to 800 ppm. Among all the groups, the one treated with CDs at the concentration of 600 ppm significantly increased both shoot (P < 0.001) and root (P < 0.001) length when compared with the control group. The SE of Mean varied from 0.47381 to 1.2495 with an increased trend and F-value of 21.3983 for shoot growth; whereas for the root growth, the SE of Mean varied from 0.32146 to 0.60914 and the F-value of 21.62536. CDs Priming may induce the development of a stronger shoot and root system, which are essential in promoting seedling growth. This effect was observed from its germination level to the strengthened shoot and root systems.

Fresh shoot and root weights for the 600 ppm group are 1.41 and 1.92 times higher than the control, respectively (Fig. 8 e). Dry biomass increased by 1.76 and 3.25 times for shoots and roots, respectively, in the 600 ppm group compared to the control (Fig. 8 f). Fig. 8 (e & f) show that CDs priming can accelerate seedling biomass accumulation, which is a crucial index during the plant growth process. Based on the measurements of fresh and dry biomass, the moisture level of the shoot and root was also influenced significantly. With the increase in the number of days, the accumulation of fresh biomass increased up to 70 %, whereas in the dry mass, there is an increase of about 20 % in both in the root and shoot systems compared to their respective control.

CDs treatment at 600 ppm significantly enhanced root branching compared to the control (Figure S5a). By Day 5, root branches numbered approximately 17 in the 600 ppm group, compared to 10 in the control. By Day 7, root branching reached 27 in the 600 ppm group, 1.93 times higher than the control. The Vigor Index values further highlight the impact of CDs on seed germination and growth. Vigor Index I, dependent on average root length, is highest at 600 ppm, indicating maximum vigor. Similarly, Vigor Index II, calculated from germination ability and dry seedling weight, is optimized at 600 ppm (Figure S5b). Figure S5 c & d illustrates the effect of different carbon dot (CDs) concentrations (200–1000 ppm) on the average shoot and root length in comparison to the control. The results indicate that moderate CDs concentrations (200–600 ppm) significantly enhance both shoot and root elongation, suggesting their role as effective nano-priming agents in seed germination. The increase in shoot length at lower concentrations may be attributed to improved water uptake, nutrient absorption, and metabolic activation, promoting early plant growth. Similarly, root elongation follows a comparable trend, with enhancement observed at moderate concentrations, potentially due to improved auxin regulation and nutrient availability. However, at higher CDs concentrations (800–1000 ppm), both shoot and root growth decline, likely due to oxidative stress or nanoparticle accumulation interfering with normal cellular processes. Compared to the control, CDs-treated seeds show improved plant improvement growth (PIG), highlighting the potential of CDs in agricultural applications. However, excessive exposure may have inhibitory effects, emphasizing the importance of optimizing CDs concentration for practical use.

Roots from the 600 ppm CDs-treated group showed significantly higher water content than the control (Figure S5e), while shoot moisture levels are similar across all groups ( Table 4). Even at the highest concentration (1000 ppm), CDs-treated seedlings exhibited better performance than the control, demonstrating the low phytotoxicity of sugarcane bagasse-derived CDs [81]. The observed dose-dependent effects suggest that higher concentrations of CDs interact more effectively with seeds, enhancing growth. However, concentrations above 600 ppm may reach a saturation point, slightly reducing the benefit. Based on these findings, 600 ppm is considered the optimal concentration for promoting seed germination and seedling development. Photosynthetic pigments are crucial for absorbing and transmitting light energy during photosynthesis, directly influencing photosynthetic rates. Chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, total chlorophyll (a+b), and chlorophyll a/b ratios are measured in mung bean leaves after treatment [82]. As shown in Figure S5f and Table 5, the 600 ppm CDs treatment significantly increased pigment content compared to the control. Chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, total chlorophyll, and the chlorophyll a/b ratio increased by 1.80, 1.72, 1.78, and 1.05 times, respectively. These results demonstrate enhanced photosynthetic efficiency under the 600 ppm treatment, correlating with improved seed germination, growth, and biomass. Overall, sugarcane bagasse-derived CDs significantly enhance seed germination and seedling development in mung bean through nano-priming. The optimal concentration of 600 ppm promotes faster germination, greater biomass accumulation, enhanced root branching, and increased photosynthetic pigment content. These findings underscore the potential of CDs as a sustainable, efficient tool for agricultural applications.

On the basis of above observation, CDs remarkably affect the growth of mung bean sprouts. Water is a main factor for seed germination. Before cellar metabolism, seeds need to uptake plenty of water. To study the role of CDs in the acceleration of germination and growth of mung bean sprouts, the moisture level of the mung bean seeds was measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) heating from 25 °C to 250 °C. The moisture level of dry mung bean seeds was measured firstly served as reference. Fig. 9 a is the mass loss curve of mung bean seeds incubated with CDs (0.02 mg/mL) and pure water, respectively. The inset of Fig. 9 a is the moisture level of seeds, revealing that 33.1 %, 40.0 % and 54.9 % of moisture level in dry seeds, seeds exposed to pure water and CDs, respectively. Obviously, there is an increase in moisture level of mung bean seeds treated with CDs, indicating that CDs can promote the water uptake capacity of seeds. Probably, CDs may improve the penetrability of seed coats and then enhance process the water absorption and accelerate the development of mung bean sprouts. To study the influence of CDs on material metabolism and energy conversion of plants, such as the photosynthesis, a series of experiments were carried out. The content of carbohydrate in the leaves of mung bean sprouts was measured firstly. Carbohydrates is the main product of photosynthesis, which provide material and energy for the development of plants. As shown in Fig. 9 b, the carbohydrates content of mung bean sprouts treated with CDs is increased 29.2 % compared to the control, indicating that the CDs treatment can enhance the formation of carbohydrates in plants. The generation of carbohydrates is related to the plant’s photosynthesis. To gain deeper insight into the role of CDs in photosynthesis, the photosystem activity of mung bean sprouts was evaluated. Photosystems are functional units within chloroplasts that mediate the primary photochemical reactions of photosynthesis, capturing light energy and facilitating electron transfer. As shown in Fig. 9 c, treatment with CDs enhanced photosystem I (PS1) activity in the leaves by 12.3 % compared to the control. In contrast, Fig. 9 d shows that photosystem II (PS2) activity remained largely unaffected by the presence of CDs. Previous studies have demonstrated that CDs can act as effective electron donors and acceptors [83], thereby promoting electron transfer in certain enzyme-catalysed reactions [84]. We speculate that CDs may accelerate electron transfer within photosystems due to these properties. As illustrated in Fig. 9 e and f, the electron transport rate of PS1 (ETR1) increased by 8.8 % in the presence of CDs, while no significant change was observed in the electron transport rate of PS2 (ETR2). PS1 contains Fe-S proteins that function as electron carriers, which are absent in PS2 [85]. Supporting this, Figure S6a shows a decrease in photoluminescence (PL) intensity for CDs-Fe complexes compared to pure CDs, suggesting the formation of non-covalent interactions between CDs and Fe2+ ions. Additionally, the PL lifetime of CDs-Fe (0.49 ns) was shorter than that of CDs alone (0.58 ns), as shown in Figure S6b, indicating an electron transfer process between CDs and Fe. Based on these findings, we propose that CDs may associate with Fe-S proteins in PS1, facilitating electron transfer and thereby enhancing PS1 activity [86].

Chlorophyll is a key pigment involved in photosynthesis, responsible for converting light energy into chemical energy. An increase in chlorophyll content enhances light absorption efficiency and promotes carbohydrate synthesis during photosynthesis. Chlorophyll levels were measured using a chlorophyll meter. As shown in Fig. 9 g, the chlorophyll content in mung bean sprouts treated with CDs increased by up to 15.8 % compared to the control, suggesting that the presence of CDs may promote chlorophyll biosynthesis. Rubisco, a key enzyme in the photosynthetic carbon cycle, is located in the chloroplasts and plays a central role in CO2 fixation. Its activity directly influences the rate of photosynthesis and the accumulation of carbohydrates [87]. The Rubisco activity in mung bean sprouts was measured. As shown in Fig. 9 h, sprouts treated with CDs exhibited a 31.1 % increase in Rubisco activity compared to the control. Since photosynthesis is a complex physiological process influenced by multiple factors, these findings collectively suggest that CDs may participate in enhancing photosynthetic efficiency and carbohydrate production by modulating photosystem activity, chlorophyll synthesis, and Rubisco activity.

To further investigate the interaction between CDs and Rubisco, we examined their effects on Rubisco activity and secondary structure in vitro. As shown in Fig. 10 a, CDs significantly enhanced Rubisco activity, while Fig. 10 b indicates that CDs also influenced the enzyme’s secondary structure. This suggests that the increased activity may result from structural changes induced by the presence of CDs. Overall, our findings demonstrate that CDs, at an optimal concentration, can promote both the growth and photosynthetic efficiency of mung bean sprouts. We separately examined their roles in plant growth and photosynthesis. Plant growth is influenced by various factors, including water uptake. CDs improved the water absorption capacity of seeds by 36.1 % compared to the control, which likely accelerated seed germination. Additionally, CDs increased root vigor by 34.9 %. Since roots are essential for water and nutrient absorption, enhanced root vigor likely contributed to increased uptake, resulting in improved root length, stem length, and biomass. Regarding photosynthesis, CDs were shown to promote carbohydrate production. As a complex process involving light absorption, energy conversion, electron transfer, ATP synthesis, and CO2 fixation, we focused our investigation on several key factors: photosystem activity, chlorophyll content, and Rubisco activity. First, CDs improved PS1 activity by 12.3 %, which can enhance the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis and support carbohydrate accumulation. Additionally, chlorophyll content increased by 15.8 % in the presence of CDs, enhancing light capture efficiency during the light reaction phase. Furthermore, Rubisco activity increased by 31.1 % compared to the control. As a central enzyme in CO2 fixation, higher Rubisco activity contributes directly to greater carbohydrate synthesis. Taken together, these results suggest that CDs promote photosynthetic efficiency and carbohydrate formation by enhancing photosystem activity, increasing chlorophyll content, and stimulating Rubisco activity.

The dynamic balance between reactive oxygen species (ROS) and antioxidant enzymes plays a pivotal role during seed imbibition and germination [88]. While ROS are naturally produced during plant metabolism, their excessive accumulation under abiotic or biotic stress can trigger oxidative damage or even cell death. To mitigate such stress, plants have evolved an antioxidant defense system that neutralizes ROS. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) catalyzes the conversion of superoxide radicals (O₂•⁻) into hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) and molecular oxygen (O₂), while enzymes like catalase (CAT), peroxidase (POD), and ascorbate peroxidase (APX) facilitate the breakdown of H₂O₂. In this study, seed priming with CDs (0.75 mg/mL) significantly enhanced SOD activity by 57.5 % compared to the control, suggesting an effective conversion of O₂•⁻ into H₂O₂ ( Fig. 11 a). Concurrently, the activities of POD, APX and CAT increased by 92.6 %, 56.6 %,\ and 156.4 %, respectively, in CDs-treated seeds (Fig. 11 b–d), contributing to the detoxification of excess H₂O₂ into water [89]. These findings indicate that CDs priming effectively boosts antioxidant enzyme activities. Moreover, the elevated enzyme levels suggest that CDs may promote moderate ROS generation through interactions with intracellular organelles post-penetration [90]. Although ROS overaccumulation is harmful, a controlled ROS presence can positively regulate growth by activating signaling cascades [91]. Enhanced antioxidant activity has been shown to improve water uptake, accelerate germination, and support seedling development, consistent with observations in this study. Additionally, increased SOD, CAT, and APX activities have been linked to greater salt stress tolerance in Lathyrus odoratus following silicon-based halo-priming [92]. These results highlight the potential of CDs priming to enhance plant resilience against environmental stresses, reduce reliance on agrochemicals, and promote sustainable agriculture [93]. Nonetheless, further research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms of CDs priming, particularly regarding ROS regulation and broader metabolic impacts in plants.

Among the tested concentrations, a 600 ppm dose of CDs yielded the highest germination index, reaching approximately 183 % after 7 days of exposure in mung bean seedlings. However, a slight decline in germination index was recorded at higher concentrations (800 ppm and 1000 ppm), likely due to partial inhibition of aquaporin function. This inhibition may have limited water uptake, ultimately resulting in a reduced germination index. Notably, the percentage inhibition of germination was negative at 600 ppm, which highlights the excellent biocompatibility of the synthesized CDs at this concentration. In parallel, as the concentration increased from 200 ppm to 600 ppm, the value of the Percentage Inhibition of Germination (PIG) progressively decreased. This negative PIG value at 600 ppm is indicative of optimal germination conditions and supports the conclusion that this concentration fosters the highest germination performance. Additionally, it is postulated that the CDs contribute to nutrient uptake enhancement. Their surface functional groups especially hydrophilic moieties like hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) can form hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions with essential nutrient ions such as K⁺, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+. These interactions lead to the adsorption of nutrient ions onto the surface of CDs, which may subsequently be transported into plant tissues [94]. As a result, the nutrient-enriched CDs enhance the mineral content in plants, thereby stimulating the germination process. Moreover, CDs are suggested to contribute to photosynthetic activity by facilitating electron transport in photosystem II (PSII). This function likely supports improved photosynthetic efficiency, which translates into a faster growth rate of the seedlings, as illustrated in Fig. 12 [95]. Table 6 [96–103] compares the effects of various CDs sources on seed germination parameters, including shoot and root length, fresh weight, germination ability, and growth indices, across different seed types. It highlights as synthesised CDs, influence seed growth and development.

The preservation of pears is evaluated using the dip-coating method, as schematically represented in Fig. 13 a. Pears are stored at room temperature (25 °C) and monitored daily. The pears are divided into four groups: untreated (control) and those coated with PVA, CDs@5 wt%, and PVA/CDs@5 wt% solutions (Fig. 13 b). Oxidation levels are assessed by observing the appearance of browning or spots on the pear skins.

Weight loss is monitored over the storage period (Fig. 13 c). The control pears experienced a weight loss rate of 27 ± 1.01 % after 30 days, while the PVA/CDs@5 wt% group showed only 5 ± 0.96 %, demonstrating the coating's effectiveness in reducing water loss. This reduction is due to the low oxygen and water vapor permeability of the PVA/CDs@5 wt% coating, which minimizes respiration and water evaporation during storage [104]. Hardness, a critical indicator of fruit quality, is measured at 5-day intervals (Fig. 13 d). A significant difference in hardness is observed between the groups. On the 10th day, pears in the PVA/CDs@5 wt% group exhibited the highest hardness, while the control group showed the lowest. The intermediate hardness observed in the PVA-coated pears is 3.52 N. These results indicate that the PVA/CDs@5 wt% coating effectively reduced the respiration rate and inhibited hydrolytic enzyme activity, slowing down the softening and spoilage processes [105]. Over the 30-day storage period, the control pears exhibited significant size reduction, weight loss, and fungal growth. In contrast, pears coated with PVA/CDs@5 wt% solutions showed minimal fungal growth, attributed to the CDs enhanced water solubility and antimicrobial properties. These findings confirm the PVA/CDs@5 wt% coating as a promising approach for extending the shelf life of fruits.

The antioxidant activities of the PVA-based composite films, as shown in Fig. 13 e, were evaluated using ABTS and DPPH assays. The ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities of the neat control were 8 % ± 1.22 and 6 % ± 1.57, respectively, while those of neat PVA were 25.02 % ± 1.30 and 16.13 % ± 1.67, respectively. This antioxidant activity of PVA films can be attributed to the hydroxyl groups in the PVA matrix [106]. Incorporating CDs into the PVA matrix significantly (p < 0.05) enhanced antioxidant activity, with ABTS radical scavenging activity increasing from 95.16 % ± 1.32–100 % ± 0.00 % and DPPH activity increasing from 52.82 % ± 1.34–59.45 % ± 0.83 %. This enhancement is due to the robust antioxidant properties of CDs, which neutralize free radicals through an electron transport mechanism facilitated by the electron-donating functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CDs.The study also highlighted a more pronounced antioxidant activity in the ABTS assay compared to the DPPH assay. This disparity is attributed to the water-soluble nature of the PVA matrix, which enhances the release of CDs in the hydrophilic ABTS solution. In contrast, the limited solubility of PVA in the hydrophobic DPPH solution restricts CD release, leading to comparatively lower antioxidant activity. These results underline the potential of PVA/CDs@5 wt% composite films as antioxidant packaging materials. Such active packaging can effectively mitigate oxidation-related food spoilage, prevent discoloration, and inhibit rancidity in high-fat foods, ultimately extending shelf life during storage.

A semi-quantitative sensory evaluation was conducted with 10 panelists to assess the visual and textural quality of coated and uncoated pears over time. As shown in Fig. 13 (f-h), the CDs@PVA-coated pears consistently received higher scores for color, texture, and overall acceptance compared to control and PVA-only samples. On Day 1, all samples scored similarly; however, by Day 15 and Day 20, the CDs@PVA-coated pears maintained superior sensory attributes, with color and texture scores remaining above 3.5 out of 5, while control samples declined sharply, indicating spoilage and softening. Overall acceptance also remained high for CDs@PVA-coated pears, suggesting that the coating effectively preserved the fruit's freshness, firmness, and appearance during storage. These findings suggest a favorable impact on consumer-relevant traits and support the role of CDs@PVA films in enhancing postharvest quality. Table 7 summarizes recent pear preservation strategies using various coating materials and storage conditions, highlighting key sensory parameters such as browning, firmness and weight loss [107–114]. Notably, our PVA@5 wt% CDs coating provided extended preservation (30 days) with effective control over browning and texture retention under ambient conditions.

The potential of CDs as invisible fluorescent ink for AC applications is explored. An aqueous solution of CDs is applied in pen mode to create various patterns and designs on cardboard, glass, magazine cover, plastic and craft paper. As shown in Fig. 14 a, under direct sun light, the patterns are colourless and invisible. However, when exposed to UV light (365 nm), the designs exhibited bright blue FL, which disappeared immediately when the UV lamp is turned off. This feature underscores the CDs’ capability for secure AC applications [115]. The fluorescent ink is found to be clear, durable, non-toxic, and easily removable, making it suitable for AC applications such as fluorescent pens. Stability tests showed that AC patterns on cardboard remained intact over time (Fig. 14 b). Even after 180 days of storage at room temperature, the patterns are clearly visible under UV light (Fig. 14 c), demonstrating the ink’s long-term stability.

To evaluate resilience under UV exposure, patterns created with CDs ink are subjected to intense UV light for 120 min at 30 min intervals (Fig. 14 d). The FL intensity remained stable for up to 60 min before showing a gradual decline (Fig. 14 e). Similarly, AC tags subjected to ultrasonication for 120 min showed consistent FL intensity for 60 min, followed by a gradual decrease (Fig. 14 f & g). Table 8 summarizes the photostability and anticounterfeiting properties of various CDs synthesized from different precursors, tested on diverse substrates under ageing, UV irradiation, and thermal treatments [116–122]. These results demonstrate the as synthesised CDs’ potential for secure data encoding and anticounterfeiting applications. Their ability to maintain fluorescent properties under challenging conditions makes them a promising material for AC tags and data security features [123].

CDs have emerged as highly versatile nanomaterials with exceptional luminescent properties, enabling diverse applications in agronomy, anti-counterfeiting, drug tracking, and intelligent sensing. This study presents the efficient synthesis of luminescent CDs from agricultural waste, highlighting their potential across multiple fields. The synthesized CDs exhibited remarkable selectivity for Pb2+ ions, acting as a sensitive nanoprobe for detecting free Pb2+ in concentrations ranging from 0–250 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 16.93 nM within a linear range of 0.01–50 μM. Lifetime decay analysis and UV–vis absorption studies supported a static quenching mechanism, affirming their reliability for sensing applications. In agriculture, CDs derived from sugarcane bagasse were employed as nano-priming agents for mung bean seeds, resulting in significantly improved seed germination, enhanced shoot and root growth, higher root vigor, increased chlorophyll content, and greater biomass accumulation compared to controls, showcasing the effectiveness of CDs in boosting plant growth. For food preservation, CDs incorporated into PVA coatings significantly extended the shelf life of fresh pears by reducing weight and moisture loss, inhibiting fungal growth, and maintaining the fruits' natural appearance and color for up to 30 days at room temperature. Moreover, CDs@5 wt% blue fluorescent ink demonstrated strong anti-counterfeiting potential by securely encoding sensitive data that is visible only under UV light. The ink’s durability, clarity, and non-toxicity make it ideal for secure data protection and anti-counterfeiting applications. Overall, this study emphasizes the versatile potential of CDs, especially those derived from agricultural waste, in applications such as nano-priming for agriculture, food preservation, Pb2+ sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and data security. These findings highlight sustainable, innovative uses of CDs in addressing global challenges across various sectors.
